([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Olympische Zomerspelen 1928*), officially the **Games of the IX Olympiad** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Spelen van de IXe Olympiade*), was an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") that was celebrated from 28 July to 12 August 1928 in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), Netherlands. The city of Amsterdam had previously bid for the 1920 and 1924 [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"). Still, it was obliged to give way to war-torn [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") for the [1920 Games](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") and [Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin")'s [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") for the [1924 Games](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics").

The **1928 Summer Olympics** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Olympische Zomerspelen 1928*), officially the **Games of the IX Olympiad** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Spelen van de IXe Olympiade*), was an international [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") that was celebrated from 28 July to 12 August 1928 in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), Netherlands. The city of Amsterdam had previously bid for the 1920 and 1924 [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"). Still, it was obliged to give way to war-torn [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") for the [1920 Games](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") and [Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin")'s [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") for the [1924 Games](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics").

The only other candidate city for the 1928 Olympics was [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), which would eventually be selected to host the Olympics four years later. In preparation for the [1932 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1932_Summer_Olympics "1932 Summer Olympics"), the [United States Olympic Committee](/wiki/United_States_Olympic_Committee "United States Olympic Committee") reviewed the costs and revenue of the 1928 Games. The committee reported a total cost of [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")1.183 million with receipts of US$1.165 million, giving a negligible loss of US$18,000, which was a considerable improvement over the 1924 Games.[[2]](#cite_note-Zarnowski-2)

The United States won the most gold and medals overall.

## Bidding process

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1928_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Bidding process")]

Dutch nobleman Frederik van [Tuyll](/wiki/Tuyll "Tuyll") van Serooskerken first proposed Amsterdam as the host city for the [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") in 1912, even before the [Netherlands Olympic Committee](/wiki/NOC*NSF "NOC*NSF") was established.

The Olympic Games were canceled in 1916 due to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). In 1919, the Netherlands Olympic Committee abandoned the proposal of Amsterdam in favor of their support for the nomination of [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") as host city for the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics"). In 1921, [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") was selected for the [1924 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics") on the condition that the 1928 Summer Olympics would be organized in Amsterdam. This decision, supported by the Netherlands Olympic Committee, was announced by the [International Olympic Committee](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") (IOC) on 2 June 1921.

[Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles")' bid for the 1928 Summer Olympics was unsuccessful in 1922 and again in 1923.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The city was eventually selected as host city for the [1932 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1932_Summer_Olympics "1932 Summer Olympics"), being the only bidder for that year.[[4]](#cite_note-official_report-4): p.915

## Organization and Innovations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1928_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Organization and Innovations")]

* These were the first Olympics to be organized under the [IOC presidency](/wiki/President_of_the_International_Olympic_Committee "President of the International Olympic Committee") of [Henri de Baillet-Latour](/wiki/Henri_de_Baillet-Latour "Henri de Baillet-Latour").
* These Games were the first to bear the name "Summer Olympic Games", to distinguish them from the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") (held since 1924).
* These Games were the first to feature a fixed schedule of sixteen days, which is still followed since 1984. In previous Olympics, competition had been stretched out over several months.
* The events were entirely funded through private donations with the government of the host nation providing no financial support for the first time.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5)
* The American company [Coca-Cola](/wiki/The_Coca-Cola_Company "The Coca-Cola Company") made its first appearance as an official sponsor of the Olympic Games, remaining the oldest Olympic sponsor to this day.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Ceremonies and Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1928_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ceremonies and Traditions")]

* A symbolic fire was lit for the first time during the Olympics, a tradition that continues today.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The first [Olympic flame](/wiki/Olympic_flame "Olympic flame") and torch relay, however, would not take place until the [1936 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics").
* For the first time, the parade of nations started with [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), which holds the origins of the Olympics, and ended with the host country, a tradition that has continued ever since.
* The Games were officially opened by [Prince Hendrik](/wiki/Prince_Henry,_Duke_of_Mecklenburg-Schwerin "Prince Henry, Duke of Mecklenburg-Schwerin"), consort of [Queen Wilhelmina](/wiki/Wilhelmina_of_the_Netherlands "Wilhelmina of the Netherlands"), who had authorized her husband to deputize for her.[[4]](#cite_note-official_report-4): p.294 The Queen was unable to attend the opening ceremony as she was on holiday in [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") and did not want to disrupt her trip.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This was the second time a head of state had not personally officiated at an [Olympic opening ceremony](/wiki/Olympic_Games_ceremony "Olympic Games ceremony") (the first occasion being the [1904 Games](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics") in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), which were officially opened by [David R. Francis](/wiki/David_R._Francis "David R. Francis"), the Mayor of St. Louis). The Queen had initially refused to appear at either the opening or closing ceremony; it is thought that she objected to the Netherlands hosting the 1928 Games as she considered the Olympics a demonstration of paganism.[[9]](#cite_note-9) However, she returned from Norway before the conclusion of the Games, to be present at the closing ceremony,[[10]](#cite_note-10) and she presented the first prizes at the prize distribution which was held immediately beforehand.[[4]](#cite_note-official_report-4): p.913

## Athlete highlights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1928_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Athlete highlights")]

* [Paavo Nurmi](/wiki/Paavo_Nurmi "Paavo Nurmi") of [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") won his ninth, and final, gold medal in the [10,000 m race](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_10,000_metres "Athletics at the 1928 Summer Olympics – Men's 10,000 metres").
* [Canadian](/wiki/Canadians "Canadians") athlete [Percy Williams](/wiki/Percy_Williams_(sprinter) "Percy Williams (sprinter)") exceeded expectations by winning both the [100 m](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_100_metres "Athletics at the 1928 Summer Olympics – Men's 100 metres") and [200 m](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_200_metres "Athletics at the 1928 Summer Olympics – Men's 200 metres") sprint events.
* [Crown Prince Olav](/wiki/Olav_V_of_Norway "Olav V of Norway"), who would later become King of Norway, won a gold medal in the [6 meter sailing](/wiki/Sailing_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_6_Metre "Sailing at the 1928 Summer Olympics – 6 Metre") event.
* [Pat O'Callaghan](/wiki/Pat_O%27Callaghan "Pat O'Callaghan") won the first ever medal for a newly independent [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), taking gold in the [hammer throw](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_hammer_throw "Athletics at the 1928 Summer Olympics – Men's hammer throw").
* [Mikio Oda](/wiki/Mikio_Oda "Mikio Oda") of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") won the [triple jump](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_triple_jump "Athletics at the 1928 Summer Olympics – Men's triple jump") event with a result of 15.21 m (49 ft 10.82 in), becoming the first gold medalist from an Asian country.
* [Betty Robinson](/wiki/Betty_Robinson "Betty Robinson") of the USA won the women's 100 metres in a world record time of 12.2 seconds. She was still just 16 years of age at the time.
* Algerian-born marathon runner [Boughera El Ouafi](/wiki/Boughera_El_Ouafi "Boughera El Ouafi") won a gold medal for France in the [men's marathon](/wiki/Athletics_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_marathon "Athletics at the 1928 Summer Olympics – Men's marathon").
* [Johnny Weissmuller](/wiki/Johnny_Weissmuller "Johnny Weissmuller"), who later appeared in several [Tarzan](/wiki/Tarzan "Tarzan") movies, won two gold medals in [swimming](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "Swimming at the 1928 Summer Olympics"): an individual gold in the [men's 100 m freestyle](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_100_metre_freestyle "Swimming at the 1928 Summer Olympics – Men's 100 metre freestyle"), and a team gold in the [men's 4 × 200 m freestyle relay](/wiki/Swimming_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_4_%C3%97_200_metre_freestyle_relay "Swimming at the 1928 Summer Olympics – Men's 4 × 200 metre freestyle relay").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-sr-12)
* [Germany](/wiki/Germany_at_the_Olympics "Germany at the Olympics") returned to the Olympic Games for the first time since 1912, after being banned from the 1920 and 1924 Games due to its role in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). The German team immediately finished second in the 1928 medal count.
* [South American](/wiki/South_American "South American") [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") made a definite breakthrough, as [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "Uruguay at the 1928 Summer Olympics") retained its title by defeating [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "Argentina at the 1928 Summer Olympics").
* India took its first ever gold medal in [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), beginning a streak of six consecutive gold medals in the sport.

Women's [athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)") and team [gymnastics](/wiki/Gymnastics "Gymnastics") debuted at these Olympics,[[13]](#cite_note-13) in spite of criticism. Five women's athletics events were added: 100 meters, 800 meters, high jump, discus, and 400 meter hurdles. In protest of the limited number of events, British women athletes, boycotted the Games.[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Halina Konopacka](/wiki/Halina_Konopacka "Halina Konopacka") of [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") became the first female Olympic track and field champion. Reports that the 800 meter run ended with several of the competitors being completely exhausted were widely (and erroneously) circulated. As a result, the IOC decided that women were too frail for long-distance running, and women's Olympic running events were limited to 200 meters until the 1960s.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[Tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") disappeared from the program until it reappeared in 1968 [as a demonstration sport](/wiki/Tennis_at_the_1968_Summer_Olympics "Tennis at the 1968 Summer Olympics").

These Games also included [art competitions](/wiki/Art_competitions_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "Art competitions at the 1928 Summer Olympics") in five categories: architecture, painting, sculpture, literature, and music.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) However, the IOC no longer considers these to be official medal events, so the medals awarded are not included in today's Olympic medal counts.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

## Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1928_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Venues")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Olympic_Stadium_Amsterdam_1928.jpg/250px-Olympic_Stadium_Amsterdam_1928.jpg)](/wiki/File:Olympic_Stadium_Amsterdam_1928.jpg)

The Olympisch Stadion in 1928

Fourteen sports venues were used for the 1928 Summer Olympics. The Swim Stadium was demolished in 1929.[[4]](#cite_note-official_report-4): p.193 The Het Kasteel football stadium was renovated in 1998–99. The Monnikenhuize stadium was demolished in 1950. The Schermzaal sports hall has also been demolished. The Olympic Stadium was renovated between 1996 and 2000, and is still in use. The Old Stadion was demolished in 1929 and replaced with housing in the Amsterdam area.

Many cars were expected for the Games, but Amsterdam had at most 2,000 single-car parking spaces. Consequently, several new parking sites were provided, and a special parking symbol was launched to show foreign visitors where they could park. The white P on a blue background was to become the international traffic sign for parking, and is still used today.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) [Athletics](/wiki/Athletics_(sport) "Athletics (sport)") events were held on a 400-meter track, later becoming the standard for athletics tracks.

## Participating nations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1928_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Participating nations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/1928_Summer_Olympic_games_countries.png/250px-1928_Summer_Olympic_games_countries.png)](/wiki/File:1928_Summer_Olympic_games_countries.png)

Participants

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/1928_Summer_olympics_team_numbers.png/250px-1928_Summer_olympics_team_numbers.png)](/wiki/File:1928_Summer_olympics_team_numbers.png)

Number of athletes

A total of 46 nations were represented at the Amsterdam Games. [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"), [Panama](/wiki/Panama "Panama"), and [Rhodesia](/wiki/Southern_Rhodesia "Southern Rhodesia") (now [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe")) competed at the Olympic Games for the first time. Germany returned after having been banned in 1920 and 1924.[[23]](#cite_note-GuttmannGermany-24)

The nations that participated in the previous games in Paris 1924 but were absent in Amsterdam 1928 were [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador").

At the time, [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") and [Ireland](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") were all dominions of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Rhodesia](/wiki/Southern_Rhodesia "Southern Rhodesia") were also a part of the British Empire, but were not dominions. For other sovereign states (i.e. United States, France, Argentina, Japan, etc.) and the international community as a whole (i.e. [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations")) the term [dominion](/wiki/British_Dominion "British Dominion"), used internally in the British Empire, was very ambiguous, meaning "something between a colony and state". It was only years later with the [Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") that this ambiguity would be dispelled. [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") was an [unincorporated territory](/wiki/Territory_of_the_United_States "Territory of the United States") and [commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_(U.S._insular_area) "Commonwealth (U.S. insular area)") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

## Poster

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1928_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Poster")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/4/49/1928-summer-oympics-poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:1928-summer-oympics-poster.jpg)

Official poster

The official poster for the Games displaying a running man in a white shirt was designed by Jos Rovers however the IOC never succeeded in obtaining the copyright of the image. The IOC used a different poster, with the [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") text *Olympische Spiele*, and an athlete partly covered in the [Dutch national flag](/wiki/Flag_of_the_Netherlands "Flag of the Netherlands"), holding a peace leaf in his hand. The poster was made for a German book about the Amsterdam Olympics.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

## Last surviving competitor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1928_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Last surviving competitor")]

The last living competitor of the 1928 Summer Olympics was [Carla Marangoni](/wiki/Carla_Marangoni "Carla Marangoni"), a member of the silver medal-winning [Italian](/wiki/Italy "Italy") gymnastic team who had been twelve years old during the Olympics. Marangoni died 18 January 2018, at the age of 102 as the oldest living Olympic medalist at the time of her death.[[25]](#cite_note-26)
